narrative: Your law school application was reviewed by an admissions model designed to predict the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students' academic records, test scores, and backgrounds. For you, with your undergraduate GPA of 3.30, LSAT score of 30.50, middle family income bracket, and full-time study plans, the model estimated a 72% probability that you would fail the bar exam initially—high enough to trigger an automatic rejection to protect the school's outcomes. The decision hinged mostly on your class rankings from earlier years: your Year 3 decile of 2 stood out as the strongest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, since students who passed typically ranked much higher, around the 6th decile on average. Your Year 1 decile of 3 reinforced this concern, similarly signaling lower performance relative to those who succeeded, making it the second-biggest influence toward a fail prediction. Interestingly, your LSAT score worked in your favor, pulling the odds slightly toward passing compared to typical successful students, but it wasn't enough to offset the ranking issues. While your solid undergraduate GPA actually exceeds the average for bar passers (3.24), and your full-time commitment aligns with most who succeed, these didn't carry as much weight in this case. Your middle-income background is common among passers (about 35%), so it stayed neutral. Overall, the model saw your profile as riskier than the typical accepted student, who boasts stronger early rankings and higher LSATs despite similar GPAs. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just the model's data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that positive influence, strengthening your grades in upcoming terms to improve your deciles, or gathering letters highlighting your resilience and full-time dedication. You might also explore programs with holistic reviews or appeal with evidence of your upward trajectory. With targeted improvements, you can reshape this story and reapply stronger. Keep pushing—you've already shown a strong academic foundation worth building on.
